data.title: Netflix eyes ‘Seinfeld’ studio lot at deep discount after dropping WBD bid: report
data.description:
• Netflix (NFLX) dropped out of the bidding for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), but it may now get another, smaller studio lot for a lot less money, according to a Bloomberg News report.
  • The streaming TV pioneer is said to be in talks to buy a historic Los Angeles movie studio lot for a fraction of its 2021 $1.85B sale price after lenders including Goldman Sachs Group (GS) repossessed the property, the report said.
  • The final price for Radford Studio Center, a one-time silent movie lot where TV series such as Gilligan’s Island and Seinfeld were shot, hasn’t been determined, and the deal still hasn’t closed, according to three people with knowledge of the discussions.
  • The current owner, Hackman Capital Partners, defaulted on $1.1B of debt and turned the property over to lenders led by Goldman after it was unable to reach a refinancing deal last year.
  • Netflix (NFLX), which has historically leased rather than owned real estate, has stepped up investing in studio lots. It’s currently developing a $1B studio lot in Fort Monmouth, New Jersey.

data.title: Japan posts second straight trade surplus in March but misses ¥1.1T forecast; exports hit record high
Convenience store Yesway prices $280M IPO at lower range of $20 per share
data.description:
• Convenience store operator Yesway has raised $280M in a U.S. IPO, selling 14M shares at $20 each, pricing at the lower end of its marketed range of $20 to $23.
  • Founded in 2015 by Brookwood Financial Partners, Yesway (YSWY) operates more than 400 convenience stores across nine U.S. states.
  • The Fort Worth, Texas-based company has an implied valuation of about $1.21B.
  • The shares are expected to begin trading on Nasdaq under the ticker symbol "YSWY" on April 22, 2026.
  • The offering is expected to close on April 23, 2026.
  • Banks, including Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan, and Goldman Sachs are acting as bookrunners.
  • Yesway (YSWY) had initially pursued an IPO as early as 2021 but delayed plans in 2022 due to volatile market conditions.

data.title: Musk's SpaceX weighs $60B buyout of Cursor or $10B partnership payout
data.description:
SpaceX (SPACE) Tuesday revealed that Cursor has granted the company an option to acquire the firm later this year in a deal valued at $60B, or alternatively pay $10B tied to their joint work.

"The combination of Cursor’s leading product and distribution to expert software engineers with SpaceX’s million H100 equivalent Colossus training supercomputer will allow us to build the world’s most useful models," Elon ​Musk's rocket maker said in a post on X.

"SpaceXAI and @cursor_ai are now working closely together to create the world’s best coding and knowledge work AI," the post said.

The move follows reports that xAI (X.AI), which is now a part of SpaceX (SPACE), may rent computing capacity to AI startup Cursor amid soaring demand.

SpaceX (SPACE) experienced a wave of developments this week as it pushes ahead toward what could become the largest initial public offering ever. The rocket and spacecraft manufacturer is targeting a listing valuation of roughly $1.75T with a $75B raise.

Meanwhile, Cursor is reportedly in talks to raise a $2B fundraising round at an over $50B valuation.

Last year in November, Cursor raised about $2.3B at a $29.3B post-money valuation in a Series D funding round, which included existing investors, including Accel, Thrive, and Andreessen Horowitz, and new partners Coatue, Nvidia (NVDA), and Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google.

data.title: Capital One outlines $2.5B Discover synergies by mid ’27 while expecting ~40 bps CET1 impact from Brex
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Capital One (COF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "In the first quarter, Capital One earned $2.2 billion or $3.34 per diluted common share." (Chief Financial Officer Andrew Young) He added, "Net of these adjusting items, first quarter earnings per share were $4.42," and said revenue declined 2% sequentially while noninterest expense declined 9%.

  • "Our provision for credit losses was roughly flat at $4.1 billion in the quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Young) He specified that the provision included about $3.8 billion of net charge-offs and "an allowance build of $230 million" to an allowance balance of $23.6 billion.

  • "We closed our acquisition of Brex shortly after the quarter closed." (Chief Financial Officer Young) He said "The consideration paid to shareholders was approximately $4.5 billion" and that the transaction "is expected to decrease the CET1 ratio by a little over 40 basis points in the second quarter."

  • "In the first quarter, the Domestic Card business posted another quarter of top line growth and strong credit results." (Founder, Chairman, CEO & President Richard Fairbank) He said purchase volume growth was 40% year-over-year, adding, "Excluding Discover year-over-year purchase volume growth was about 8%."

  • "In closing, first quarter results continued to reflect solid top line growth and strong credit performance." (CEO Fairbank) He also said, "We made expected progress on the Discover integration and synergies in the quarter, including the successful conversion of Capital One's debit customers to the Discover Network," and "We remain on track to deliver the expected synergies."

  • "We also launched the new Capital One Travel app" after bringing travel technology in-house, and "the Hopper talent we've worked with will join Capital One." (CEO Fairbank) He said, "our numbers starting in the second quarter will include Brex and the in-sourcing of our travel business as well."

Outlook

  • "The legacy Discover card loans continued to contract slightly and will likely continue to face a temporary growth headwind in the near term due to Discover's prior credit policy cutbacks and some additional credit policy changes we've made since closing the acquisition." (CEO Fairbank) He said Capital One "continue[s] to see good opportunities to grow the Discover Card business on the other side of our tech integration."

  • "We expect to have fully transitioned new originations by the end of Q3." (CEO Fairbank) He also said, "we expect that the back book of existing Discover accounts will be fully converted onto our platform by the first quarter of next year."

  • "We do make or are making some progress on the expense synergies along the way." (Chief Financial Officer Young) He added, "you should expect that we won't be fully at our expense synergies until the conversions are complete and that will be in the first half of '27," and reiterated, "we still feel very good about achieving the full $2.5 billion of synergies by the time we complete integration in the middle of '27."

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management’s forward-looking emphasis shifted from announcing the Brex agreement to outlining post-close integration accounting timing and incremental investment posture: "we will be leveraging Capital One assets and increasing investment levels to drive enhanced growth at Brex." (CEO Fairbank)

Financial Results

  • "Our first quarter net interest margin was 7.87%, 39 basis points lower than the prior quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Young) He attributed the decline to "2 fewer days" (18 basis points), "the normal seasonal effect of lower average card balances," and elevated average cash levels.

  • "Total liquidity reserves ended the first quarter at about $165 billion, up about $21 billion from the prior quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Young) He said cash ended at approximately $76 billion and cited "continued strong deposit growth" and seasonal card balance paydowns; the "preliminary average liquidity coverage ratio was 166%."

  • "Our common equity Tier 1 capital ratio ended the quarter at 14.4%, 10 basis points higher than the fourth quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Young) He said it was partially offset by "$2.5 billion in share repurchases."

  • "Total company marketing expense in the quarter was about $1.5 billion" and CEO Fairbank said the year-over-year increase was driven by Discover plus higher legacy direct marketing, higher media, and premium benefits, adding that first quarter marketing was seasonally low and some planned spending shifted into later quarters.

Q&A

  • Terry Ma, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about consumer health amid energy price concerns; CEO Fairbank replied, "the U.S. consumer remained healthy and the overall economy remained resilient," while warning, "the new conflict in the Persian Gulf represents a significant cloud on the horizon" and said elevated risk was incorporated into the allowance through qualitative factors.

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: asked how to think about the efficiency ratio as Brex and Hopper enter the run rate; CEO Fairbank said marketing will be heavier through the year and stated, "we still expect our earnings power on the other side of the Discover integration to be consistent with what we expected at the time we announced the deal, inclusive of the Brex and the Hopper travel infrastructure."

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: asked about elevated liquidity and NIM; CFO Young said, "I do expect that, that cash position will trend down over time," citing "about $8 billion of debt maturities in Q2" and tax payments as offsets.

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: pressed for efficiency parameters and clarity on “earnings power”; CEO Fairbank said, "we're talking about ROTCE" and "The capital level assumed in the deal model was 12.5%."

  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen, Research Division: asked about card growth and Discover “brownout”; CEO Fairbank said Discover outstandings were down 1.2% year-over-year and described the brownout as temporary, adding conversion timing for originations and the back book.

  • L. Erika Penala, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: asked about Basel III endgame and capital actions; CFO Young said the standardized approach could raise CET1 "by something like 20 basis points" on a fully phased-in basis today, reflecting an "8% to 9% decrease" in RWA and about "$5.2 billion of AOCI" as a headwind if fully phased in.

  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities: asked about incremental M&A after Brex; CEO Fairbank said, "we are not at all focused on buying banks" and described a preference for "smaller tech companies."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, with repeated pressure on efficiency, “earnings power” transparency, and capital returns, including Nash’s comment that the issue "has certainly weighed on the stock" and Martinez pressing why buybacks were not higher.

  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks emphasizing integration progress and investment, but more defensive in Q&A around guidance granularity, including Fairbank’s explanation that the company is "not... in the guidance business" and repeated reliance on being "consistent with what we expected" on the Discover earnings-power framework.

  • Versus the prior quarter, analyst scrutiny shifted from Brex deal mechanics to post-close impacts (efficiency run rate, NIM/cash normalization, and ROTCE framing), while management shifted from announcing the Brex agreement to executing (close completed) and describing integration timelines.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The Brex narrative moved from "entered into a definitive agreement to acquire Brex" (Q4 2025) to post-close execution: "we closed our acquisition of Brex" with $4.5 billion consideration and a stated ~40 basis point CET1 impact in Q2 (Q1 2026).

  • Discover integration milestones advanced quarter-over-quarter: Q4 emphasized the debit conversion was "nearly complete"; Q1 stated the debit conversion was "completed" and laid out more detailed credit conversion timing (originations by end of Q3; back book by Q1 next year).

  • Capital return posture stayed consistent at $2.5 billion of buybacks, but Q1 brought more direct analyst pushback on why repurchases were not higher given the 14.4% CET1 ratio.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Fairbank flagged macro risk from geopolitics and energy: "the new conflict in the Persian Gulf represents a significant cloud on the horizon," adding that sustained high energy prices could be "a real headwind for consumers" and "a drag on the overall macro economy."

  • CFO Young highlighted NIM sensitivity to elevated cash and seasonality, while indicating normalization: "the direction of travel for cash should be down from here."

  • Discover integration risk remains tied to timing of technology conversions and back-loaded expense synergies; CFO Young said full expense synergies depend on platform conversions completing in the first half of ’27.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a quarter of "solid top line growth and strong credit performance" while advancing two integration arcs that investors are tracking closely: Discover’s platform conversion (with expense synergies "more backloaded" until the first half of ’27 and a $2.5 billion synergy target by mid-’27) and Brex’s post-close incorporation (a stated ~$4.5 billion consideration and an expected little-over-40-basis-point CET1 impact in Q2). Executives also emphasized higher planned investment levels—including marketing shifting into later quarters and incremental spend to accelerate Brex—while reiterating that "earnings power" expectations on the far side of Discover integration remain unchanged under their ROTCE framework.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wednesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
7:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications

The Mortgage Bankers' Association compiles various mortgage loan indexes. The purchase applications index measures applications at mortgage lenders.

10:00 AM Atlanta Fed Business Inflation Expectations

The Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey provides a monthly measure of year-ahead inflation expectations and inflation uncertainty from the perspective of firms. 

10:30 AM EIA Petroleum Status Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on petroleum inventories in the U.S., whether produced here or abroad. 

1:00 PM 20-Yr Bond Auction 

Treasury bonds are sold at regularly scheduled public auctions. The competitive bids at these auctions determine the interest rate paid on each Treasury bond issue.

data.title: W. R. Berkley signals shift toward growth vs. rate as Q1 2026 ROE reaches 21.2%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: W. R. Berkley (WRB) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “This continues to be very much a cyclical industry…these days, it would seem as though the fear is fading and the greed is fully percolating in many of the corners of the marketplace today,” said (CEO W. Robert Berkley, Jr.), while flagging “a notable shift in the appetite of the standard market…national carriers who seem to be broadening their appetite.”
  • Berkley highlighted rapid competitive change in reinsurance and property: “we have been taken aback a bit by the pace of change,” and added on insurance that “we are definitely seeing that, particularly with cat exposed property.”
  • Berkley outlined line-by-line posture: “GL and umbrella…rate is still available,” “D&O…continuing to flirt with the bottom,” “California…workers’ compensation…out in front,” and “auto would continue to be an area of great concern.”
  • Baio framed the quarter’s key financial messages: “record net investment income and strong underwriting profits contributing to a return on beginning of year stockholders’ equity of 21.2%,” said (Executive VP & CFO Richard Baio), adding, “net income for the quarter was $515 million or $1.31 per share, while record operating income was $514 million or $1.30 per share.”
  • Berkley pointed to pricing/retention and a strategic pivot: “rate came in…7.2% ex comp,” “the renewal retention ratio continues to sit at around 80%,” and “we are actively rethinking what the balance is between rate versus growth…you may see us take our foot slightly off the rate pedal.”

Outlook

  • No formal EPS or revenue guidance was provided in the prepared remarks.
  • Berkley described an internal shift in emphasis rather than a numeric target: “there is a reasonable chance that we will see a bit more growth as the year unfolds,” and “as we identify pockets where we are willing to make a trade… it takes a little bit of time for that to come into focus.”
  • Baio reiterated cost expectations: “We continue to believe that the 2026 expense ratio will be comfortably below 30%, barring any material changes in the marketplace.”
  • Compared with last quarter’s messaging on rate pressure easing, Berkley now made the trade-off explicit: “the need for rate is perhaps not going to be as strong going forward…actively rethinking what the balance is between rate versus growth.”

Financial results

  • Operating income was $1.30 per share vs. $1.14 (analysts’ Q1 2026 EPS estimate).
  • Baio reported underwriting profitability and cats: “current accident year combined ratio, excluding cat losses, was 88.3%, and the calendar year combined ratio was 90.7%,” with “current accident year cat losses…$76 million.”
  • Baio described premium trends amid competition: “the insurance segment grew gross premiums written by 4.5% to $3.4 billion and net premiums written by 3.2% to $2.8 billion,” while “the reinsurance and monoline access segment reported net premiums written of $395 million, reflecting decreases in property and casualty lines.”
  • Investment income was a key swing factor: “net investment income increased 12.2% to a record $404 million,” including “core portfolio…$354 million” and “investment fund income…$40 million,” said (Executive VP & CFO Baio), adding, “we report the investment funds under 1 quarter lag.”
  • Baio highlighted a nonrecurring tax item: “we reflected a net nonrecurring tax benefit, reducing our effective tax rate from 22.8% to 16.3% as reported,” and said, “We expect the remainder of 2026 will return to our normalized run rate.”
  • Capital return remained active: “we repurchased approximately 4.5 million common shares amounting to $302 million and paid regular dividends of $34 million,” while “stockholders’ equity increased to approximately $9.75 billion.”

Q&A

  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo: Question on reconciling “more competitive” market comments with a pivot to growth; (CEO Berkley): “there are still pockets where there is good opportunity…a lot of those pockets tend to be more casualty related.”
  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs: Question on property price adequacy; (CEO Berkley): “there’s still margin… but it’s fallen off pretty quickly…most quickly in the reinsurance marketplace.”
  • Taylor Scott, Barclays: Question on reinsurance run rate and “anything funky”; (CEO Berkley): “Nothing funky… it’s just a reflection of market conditions.”
  • Andrew Kligerman, TD Cowen: Question on buybacks/dividends/leverage and “why the big buyback”; (CEO Berkley): “with the company generating…20-plus percent returns…we…will have significant amounts of capital to return to shareholders for the foreseeable.”
  • Tracy Benguigui, Wolfe: Question on whether higher investment income changes underwriting targets; (CEO Berkley): “The answer is no…we…have never subscribed to…cash flow underwriting.”
  • Joshua Shanker, BofA: Question on what’s driving the reinsurance decline and MGA competition; (CEO Berkley): “the lion’s share…would be a treaty reinsurance business…due to…appropriate pricing,” and “we are not seeing the delegated authority model…subside.”
  • Katie Sakys, Autonomous: Question on commercial auto growth vs. shrinking exposures; (CEO Berkley): “the growth…is premium, not unit growth…So the exposure is shrinking and the rate is increasing.”
  • Katie Sakys, Autonomous: Question on Berkley Embedded and channel conflict; (CEO Berkley): “they do have one product offering…in the consumer space,” and “we are very committed to our traditional distribution…we need to be willing to meet insured where they wish to be met.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on competition, casualty trend, reinsurance pullback, and capital return cadence, including “square away your comments” (Greenspan) and “why the big buyback” (Kligerman).
  • Management tone was confident but cautious on cycle risk and pricing discipline, including “we remain concerned for the health and well-being of that marketplace” and “we are not willing to throw the underwriting discipline out the window” (CEO Berkley).
  • Versus last quarter, management commentary shifted from technology/distribution emphasis to sharper near-term cycle warnings and competitive intensity, including “fear is fading and the greed is fully percolating” (CEO Berkley) compared with Q4’s broader change narrative.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Competition commentary intensified: Q4 described property as “a feeding frenzy…London, particularly Lloyd’s,” while Q1 added “a notable shift…national carriers…broadening their appetite” and being “taken aback…by the pace of change” in reinsurance.
  • Strategic posture evolved from easing rate pressure to an explicit growth-rate trade: Q4 said, “we are not feeling across the board the same level of pressure to keep pushing on rate,” while Q1 said, “actively rethinking…rate versus growth.”
  • Capital return remained central in both quarters; Q4 framed “return the excess capital,” while Q1 emphasized durability: “we will have significant amounts of capital to continue to return to shareholders.”

Risks and concerns

  • Berkley warned of pricing erosion and irrational competition: “greed is fully percolating,” and on reinsurance/property, “more irrational behavior…will be plentiful.”
  • Line-specific risk flags included “cat exposed property,” “EPLI…California,” “workers’ compensation…WCIRB,” and “auto…great concern…loss cost trend.”
  • On underwriting discipline as mitigation, Berkley said, “we look to each component of our economic model to stand on its own 2 feet.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a strong earnings and capital-return quarter, led by record net investment income and profitable underwriting, while repeatedly warning that competitive pressures are accelerating across property and reinsurance. At the same time, the CEO described a deliberate pivot to balance “rate versus growth,” with the company aiming to lean into select pockets where it still sees “attractive margins,” without changing underwriting targets to reflect higher investment income.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intuitive forecasts 13.5%-15.5% 2026 da Vinci procedure growth while expanding Force Feedback availability
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Q1 was a solid start to the year for Intuitive, driven by 17% total procedure growth and broad-based adoption across da Vinci and Ion" (CEO & Director David Rosa).
  • "In Q1, da Vinci procedures grew 16% to 847,000, and Ion procedures increased 39% to 43,000" (CEO & Director Rosa).
  • "In China, the environment remains largely consistent with recent quarters reflecting relatively low tender activity across the category, domestic competition and policy-driven pricing pressure" (CEO & Director Rosa).
  • "We placed 431 da Vinci systems in Q1, including 232 da Vinci 5 systems, 34 SP systems and 34 XiR systems. We also placed 52 Ion systems in the quarter" (CEO & Director Rosa).
  • "In March, we received FDA 510(k) clearance for additional uses of our Force Feedback instruments... this clearance supports broader availability in Q2 that will increase over the rest of the year" (CEO & Director Rosa).
  • "I want to recognize an important leadership transition at Intuitive. Dr. Myriam Curet is retiring this quarter" and "I'm also pleased to announce Dr. Jamie Wong's promotion to Chief Medical Officer" (CEO & Director Rosa).
  • "Quarter 1 revenue increased 23% to $2.77 billion" and "Non-GAAP operating margin was strong at 39%" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Jamie Samath).
  • "I would like to briefly address the cyber incident... The incident did not disrupt our business or manufacturing operations and did not affect our products" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).

Outlook

  • "We are increasing our forecast and now expect full year da Vinci procedure growth within a range of 13.5% to 15.5%" (VP & Head of Investor Relations Daniel Connally).
  • "We are updating our estimate for non-GAAP gross profit margin to be within a range of 67.5% and 68.5% of revenue, which now reflects 100 basis points of impact from tariffs" (VP & Head of Investor Relations Connally).
  • "In regard to operating expenses, we now expect non-GAAP operating expense growth to be between 11% and 14%" (VP & Head of Investor Relations Connally).
  • "We now forecast other income... to total between $315 million and $335 million due primarily to lower average cash balances following share repurchase activity in Q1" (VP & Head of Investor Relations Connally).

Financial Results

  • "Quarter 1 revenue increased 23% to $2.77 billion, with recurring revenue also higher by 23% to $2.4 billion, accounting for 86% of total revenue" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • "Non-GAAP gross margin for the quarter was 67.8%" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • "Non-GAAP net income for the first quarter was $901 million" and "Non-GAAP earnings per share was $2.50 per share" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • "GAAP net income for the quarter was $822 million or $2.28 per share" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • "We ended the quarter with $8 billion in cash and investments... driven by stock repurchases of $1.1 billion, the acquisition of our distributor business in Italy, Spain and Portugal, and capital expenditures of $103 million" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).

Q&A

  • Travis Steed, BofA Securities, Research Division: asked for timing and the “big picture view” on digital/data, AI, and ROSE/biopsy; CEO & Director David Rosa said, "our approach here is... to build kind of this layered capabilities" starting with "high-quality data" and that "I believe that gives us the foundation to strengthen the differentiation over the next 3 to 5 years," and added on ROSE/EBUS, "The time lines are more short term, but they won't be this year" (CEO & Director Rosa).
  • Travis Steed, BofA Securities, Research Division: pressed on gross margin bridge and macro inputs; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Jamie Samath said, "based on what we know today, in the gross margin guidance, it has an impact, but it's relatively small" and pointed to "relatively significant leverage from the 23% revenue growth" and "product cost reductions" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about sizing appendectomy and Japan upside; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Jamie Samath said, "We haven't sized appendectomy yet" and noted it is "typically a relatively quick procedure with relatively low reimbursements"; VP & Head of Investor Relations Daniel Connally said Japan’s largest new procedure is bilateral inguinal hernia repair and "it's too early for us to size the incremental procedure opportunity in Japan" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath; VP & Head of Investor Relations Connally).
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about “innovation-led revenue growth”; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath described a "push and pull" where Intuitive shares differentiated value via "accretive pricing" (including "da Vinci 5") while also sharing cost savings in cost-sensitive settings, adding, "we don't guide revenue" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked how much further utilization can go and the placement impact; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath replied, "that's the impossible question to answer" and said U.S. utilization is expected to be influenced by how fast the installed base "switches over to da Vinci 5" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked about competition and tiered pricing with refurbished Xi; CEO & Director Rosa said competition is about "meeting the needs of the customer at the right price point" and emphasized program value; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath added, "the segmentation there is really appealing" and called refurbished Xi "a very capable product" (CEO & Director Rosa; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • Frederick Wise, Stifel, Nicolaus & Company, Incorporated, Research Division: asked why OUS I&A outpaced OUS procedures; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath said it was likely "customer ordering pattern" and that distributors "can be pretty lumpy" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • Frederick Wise, Stifel, Nicolaus & Company, Incorporated, Research Division: asked about telesurgery and longer-term adjacencies; CEO & Director Rosa said telesurgery "is on our road map" and discussed telecollaboration adoption, saying "we're seeing thousands of use cases a month" (CEO & Director Rosa).
  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked whether SP is TAM-expanding and pace of acceleration; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath said, "those opportunities exist" and cited "Nipple-sparing mastectomy" as an example, while noting "the next year or 2 is -- that's where our focus is" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked how Intuitive is prioritizing OUS markets amid lower-cost entrants; CEO & Director Rosa said the strategy is "all of the above" across people, portfolio, pricing, and multiyear market access work, and Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath added, "we take a localized approach" and noted "localized manufacturing" may matter in some markets (CEO & Director Rosa; Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative: analysts repeatedly pressed on durability (utilization), competition and pricing (China/tiered portfolio), and the sustainability of revenue-per-procedure dynamics, with a skeptical edge in questions like "how much more is there to go?" (Robert Marcus, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division).
  • Slightly positive: management emphasized momentum and longer-term road maps, using confident framing such as "Q1 was a solid start" and "I have confidence in our ability to execute our international strategy" (CEO & Director David Rosa).
  • Versus last quarter, the discussion shifted from Q4’s emphasis on ASCs/cardiac groundwork to Q1’s heavier focus on AI/digital layers and near-term product availability, while keeping similar caution language on China/Japan and U.S. policy sensitivity (CEO & Director Rosa; VP & Head of Investor Relations Daniel Connally).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "We are increasing our forecast" for 2026 da Vinci procedure growth, moving from the prior range of "13% and 15%" to "13.5% to 15.5%" (VP & Head of Investor Relations Daniel Connally).
  • Q1’s call added a disclosed operational issue and reassurance around it, with "the cyber incident" described as contained and not disruptive, a topic not raised in the prior quarter’s prepared remarks (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Jamie Samath).
  • Management expanded detail on monetizable performance drivers, tying mix and trade-ins to "accretive pricing" in da Vinci 5 and highlighting refurbishment/segmentation more explicitly than in Q4’s framing (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • Analysts’ focus rotated from Q4’s ASC and cardiac clearance questions toward Q1’s AI roadmap, margin building blocks, and competitive segmentation, while continuing to probe China and utilization (Travis Steed, BofA Securities, Research Division; Robert Marcus, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division).

Risks and Concerns

  • "The lower growth rate relative to prior quarters reflects ongoing challenges in China and Japan" (CEO & Director David Rosa).
  • "We do not expect clarity on the outcome of these matters until 2027" regarding China charge code and reimbursement discussions (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Jamie Samath).
  • "We remain cautious in our outlook for the Japanese market in the short term given the financial position of public hospitals" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • "We remain cautious as to what the potential impact, if any, might be" from ACA premium subsidy expiration (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).
  • "Future gross margins will reflect... competitive pricing dynamics, global tariff rates and product, regional and trade-in mix" (Executive VP, CFO & Enterprise Technology Leader Samath).

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 as a utilization- and mix-driven start to 2026, highlighted da Vinci 5 adoption, Force Feedback instruments moving toward broader Q2 availability, and accelerating SP and Ion procedure growth, while raising the full-year da Vinci procedure-growth outlook range and tightening gross margin expectations. Executives also underscored continued caution around China tenders and Japan capital constraints, and disclosed a contained cyber incident that did not disrupt operations, as the company emphasized an AI-enabled digital roadmap and a tiered portfolio that includes refurbished Xi systems.
